serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of cream cheese spread is approximately 1 tablespoon (15g), commonly used as a spread on bread, bagels, or crackers.

nutrient_source.title: Cream cheese, spread — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo Database entry for cream cheese spread (F01010012). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this approved primary source.

gi_source.title: N/A — Cream cheese spread contains negligible carbohydrates (3.5g/100g, predominantly lactose); glycemic index is not applicable for high-fat, very low-carbohydrate dairy spreads
gi_source.source_match_notes: Cream cheese spread has only 3.5g carbohydrate per 100g, of which all is sugar (lactose). The University of Sydney GI Database does not list a GI value for cream cheese spread as the carbohydrate content is too low to elicit a meaningful glycemic response. Per Project Veritas v2 rules for negligible-carbohydrate foods (cream cheese is explicitly listed), glycemic_index is set to 0 as a numeric sentinel indicating not-applicable status, gi_category is 'not_applicable', and glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0. Glycemic load per serving (15g) is effectively 0.0 as carbohydrate contribution per serving is 0.53g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (436mg/100g) exceeding the 400mg/100g threshold, combined with high saturated fat (~18g/100g) contributing to arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit cream cheese spread due to its sodium content of 436mg/100g; if used, restrict to no more than 1 teaspoon (5g) per occasion and choose reduced-sodium, reduced-fat variants where available.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (3.5g/100g) poses minimal glycemic risk, but high caloric density (295 kcal/100g) and saturated fat load may worsen insulin resistance and promote weight gain
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cream cheese spread does not significantly raise blood glucose due to its very low carbohydrate content (3.5g/100g), but individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit portions to 15–20g per serving to avoid excess caloric and saturated fat intake that may worsen insulin resistance; avoid pairing with high-GI carriers such as white bread or crackers.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat content (~18g/100g) raises LDL cholesterol; cholesterol content of 90mg/100g and sodium of 436mg/100g further elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Cream cheese spread is high in saturated fat (~18g/100g) and cholesterol (90mg/100g), both of which are established risk factors for cardiovascular disease; individuals with CVD or elevated LDL should minimize consumption and substitute with lower-fat dairy spreads such as low-fat ricotta or reduced-fat cottage cheese.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~18g/100g greatly exceeds the 5g/100g RA threshold; pro-inflammatory fatty acid profile with no meaningful anti-inflammatory components
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Cream cheese spread is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its high saturated fat content (~18g/100g), which is strongly pro-inflammatory; replace with low-fat Greek yogurt or hummus as an anti-inflammatory spread alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cream cheese spread is a softened, processed dairy product made primarily from cream cheese, milk fat, and milk solids, with added salt. It has a smooth, creamy, and easily spreadable texture that distinguishes it from block cream cheese. The flavour is mildly tangy and rich, with a subtle saltiness. It is widely used as a spread on bagels, toast, crackers, and sandwiches, and as a base for dips and savoury toppings. Per 100g, it provides 295 kcal, 7.1g protein, 28.6g fat (of which approximately 18g is saturated fat), 3.5g carbohydrate (all as sugar, predominantly lactose), 0g dietary fibre, and 436mg sodium. Cholesterol is 90mg/100g. The high fat and sodium content make it a food to be consumed in moderation, particularly for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. Its negligible carbohydrate content means it does not significantly raise blood glucose, making it relatively suitable for type 2 diabetes in small portions. Cream cheese spread is classified as a processed dairy product and is a source of calcium (71mg/100g) and potassium (112mg/100g). It is vegetarian and gluten-free but not suitable for vegans or those with dairy allergies. A typical serving is 1 tablespoon (approximately 15g). Its preparation involves blending and softening cream cheese, often with stabilizers, to achieve its characteristic spreadable consistency. The rich, dairy-forward taste makes it a popular condiment in Western and international cuisine.

confidence_reason: All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo Database (F01010012), an approved primary source with confidence 0.95. The record is an exact match to the input data. Saturated fat is estimated at ~18g/100g based on typical cream cheese composition cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (NDB 01017) as a secondary validation, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry.
notes: Saturated fat (18.0g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for cream cheese (NDB 01017, SR Legacy) as HPB does not separately report this value; this is a secondary validation and the HPB value remains primary for all other nutrients. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these values are not reported in the HPB Foodo Database entry for cream cheese spread (F01010012) and detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data are not available in the HPB source as of 2026-04-16. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 as confirmed by HPB source — cream cheese spread contains no dietary fibre. Cream cheese spread; glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (3.5g/100g). glycemic_index is set to numeric 0 as a machine-readable sentinel for not-applicable status per Q4 validator requirement that glycemic_index must be numeric; gi_category is 'not_applicable' and gi_source documents the University of Sydney GI Database consultation. Glycemic load per serving (15g) is 0.0 as carbohydrate contribution per serving is 0.53g. No zero-macro justification required as protein_g (7.1), fat_g (28.6), and carbohydrate_g (3.5) are all non-zero.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.0 confirmed by HPB), sodium_mg (436.0), saturated_fat_g (18.0 estimated from USDA SR Legacy NDB 01017 as secondary validation), sugar_g (3.5) all present and numeric.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index is set to numeric 0 (machine-readable sentinel for not-applicable status) satisfying the validator requirement that glycemic_index must be numeric. gi_category is 'not_applicable'. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0. gi_source documents the University of Sydney GI Database consultation and the not-applicable determination for this negligible-carbohydrate dairy product.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI FOODON_03301710 (cream cheese) is a defensible exact match. FoodEx2 code A00LC (Cream cheese) is present. SNOMED CT code 226760005 (Cream cheese substance) is present. All three anchors are defensible and consistent.

Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance. Human condition name 'Type 2 Diabetes' used correctly with machine key 'type2_diabetes'.

Nutrient data is sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo Database (primary, exact match). Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy as a secondary validation and documented in notes. All -1 sentinel values are documented. Ontology anchors are defensible. Health context covers Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and Cardiovascular Disease with specific clinical guidance. Aliases are comprehensive and internally consistent.
